memory_text: Recent Python versions introduced significant features: Python 3.5 (2015) added `async/await` syntax (PEP 492), Python 3.6 (2016) introduced f-strings (PEP 498), Python 3.8 (2019) included the walrus operator (`:=`) (PEP 572), and Python 3.10 (2021) brought structural pattern matching (PEP 634) with `match/case` statements.

Ada Lovelace is often regarded as the first computer programmer due to her contributions, including developing the concept of looping instructions for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine and recognizing its importance as a general-purpose computing device.

Augusta Ada King, Countess of Lovelace, born on 10 December 1815, was an English mathematician and writer known for her work on Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine and is often regarded as the first computer programmer for recognizing the machine's applications beyond pure calculation and publishing the first algorithm intended to be processed by a machine.

Ada Lovelace's work was not widely known until Alan Turing cited her 1843 paper in his 1950 paper "Computing Machinery and Intelligence," leading to the US Department of Defense naming the "Ada" programming language after her in 1980 and the establishment of Ada Lovelace Day in the United Kingdom.

Guido van Rossum served as Python's Benevolent Dictator For Life (BDFL), a title given to him by the Python community, until July 2018, when he stepped down from the role which granted him final say over all Python Enhancement Proposals (PEPs) and the direction of the language.

The Python Software Foundation (PSF), a non-profit organization founded in 2001, holds intellectual property rights for the Python programming language, organizes PyCon, the annual Python conference, and manages the Python Package Index (PyPI).

Ada Lovelace was tutored by William Frend, William King, and Mary Somerville, and at age 17, she corresponded with and met Charles Babbage, who subsequently introduced her to his mentor Mary Somerville, through whom she met other scientists including Michael Faraday.

The Analytical Engine was a proposed mechanical general-purpose computer designed by English mathematician Charles Babbage, first described in 1837 as the successor to his Difference Engine, incorporating an arithmetic logic unit, control flow in the form of conditional branching and loops, and integrated memory, making it the first design for a Turing-complete general-purpose computer.

Ada Lovelace translated Luigi Menabrea's article on Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in 1842–43 and supplemented it with "Notes," which are recognized as the first published algorithm intended for a machine, specifically containing an algorithm for computing Bernoulli numbers in Note G.

Ada Lovelace, the only legitimate child of the poet Lord Byron and Anne Isabella Milbanke, was privately educated in mathematics and science by her mother, Lady Byron, an amateur mathematician who nicknamed her "The Princess of Parallelograms."

Guido van Rossum was employed by Google from 2005 to 2012, then by Dropbox from 2013 to 2019, and joined Microsoft in 2020; he also received the Free Software Foundation Award in 2001, the NLUUG Award in 2003, and was named a Fellow of the ACM in 2020.

Python was designed for high readability, frequently using English keywords and having fewer syntactic constructions than other languages, with its design philosophy, "The Zen of Python" (PEP 20), published by Guido van Rossum in 1999, emphasizing principles like "Beautiful is better than ugly" and "Readability counts."

Python Enhancement Proposals (PEPs) are the formal mechanism for proposing, discussing, and deciding changes to Python, with notable examples including PEP 8 (Style Guide), PEP 484 (Type Hints), PEP 498 (f-strings), PEP 572 (Assignment Expressions), and PEP 634 (Structural Pattern Matching).

Python 2.0, released on 16 October 2000, introduced a cycle-detecting garbage collector, Unicode support, and list comprehensions, and its support officially ended on January 1, 2020, with users strongly encouraged to migrate to Python 3.

Python, a high-level, general-purpose programming language that emphasizes code readability using significant indentation and is dynamically typed and garbage-collected, was conceived by Guido van Rossum in the late 1980s at Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI) as a successor to the ABC programming language, with its first version (0.9.0) published in February 1991.

Charles Babbage, an English polymath born 26 December 1791, originated the concept of a digital programmable computer and is considered a "father of the computer" for inventing the first mechanical computer, the Difference Engine, and conceiving the Analytical Engine with all essential ideas of modern computers.

Python 3.0, released on 3 December 2008, was a major, backwards-incompatible revision aimed at fixing early design oversights, particularly around Unicode handling, the print function, and integer division, by changing print into a function, ensuring integer division always returns a float when appropriate, and making text strings always Unicode.

Ada Lovelace collaborated with Charles Babbage on an 1843 paper describing the Analytical Engine, in which she included what is now considered the first published algorithm—a method to compute Bernoulli numbers—specifically intended for the machine to execute.

The Analytical Engine was designed to be steam-powered and operated by one person, receiving inputs via punched cards inspired by Joseph Marie Jacquard's textile looms, and its programming was intended to use operation, variable, and number cards to enable conditional branching, looping, and subroutine calls.

Charles Babbage proposed a steam-driven Difference Engine in 1822 to compute tables of numbers; the project was canceled in 1842, but a working Difference Engine, weighing 2.6 tonnes and measuring 3.4 metres long, was eventually constructed in 1991 at the Science Museum in London using his original plans.

Guido van Rossum, a Dutch programmer born on 31 January 1956, is best known as the creator of the Python programming language, which he began developing at Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI) in December 1989 and named "Python" as a tribute to "Monty Python's Flying Circus."
The Analytical Engine was never completed, as Charles Babbage worked on it until his death in 1871, with construction only proceeding to a portion of the Mill and the printing mechanism.

Around 1834, Charles Babbage conceived the Analytical Engine, a general-purpose mechanical computer featuring a Mill (computing unit), a Store capable of holding 1,000 numbers of 50 decimal digits each, a Reader for punched card input, and a Printer, which Ada Lovelace collaborated on from 1842 onwards by writing the first algorithm intended for its execution.
